title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 2. Put on disposable gloves if your healthcare provider says you should.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 3. Look for clots or blockages that may prevent the fluid from flowing out of the tube and into the bulb. Some clots may be hidden inside the tube under your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 4. Loosen the clots by gently squeezing the tubes surrounding them.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 5. Use one hand to hold the drain tube in place where it leaves your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 6. Use your other hand to pinch the tube with an alcohol wipe between your finger and thumb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 7. Slide your pinched fingers along the tube to force any fluid out of the tube and into the bulb. You may need to repeat steps 5 through 7 several times to clear the tube. Try not to let go of the tube between steps. If fluid remains in the tube or you accidentally let go, repeat steps 5 through 7 using a new alcohol wipe.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Do not allow the bulb to become more than half full. Too much fluid in the bulb reduces its ability to remove fluid from underneath your skin. Now you will begin emptying the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 8. Hold the bulb lower than your incision so that fluid moves out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 9. Point the bulb away from your body. Never squeeze the bulb before taking the cap off.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 10. Remove the cap. Never touch the opening with your bare hands.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 11. Hold the measuring cup under the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 12. Turn the bulb upside down and squeeze the fluid into the cup.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 13. After removing the fluid, continue squeezing the bulb and use a new alcohol wipe to clean the top.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 14. While still squeezing the bulb, put the cap back on the top. The depressed bulb creates suction that continuously removes fluid from underneath your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 15. Read the amount of fluid in the measuring cup.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 16. Write the amount on your record sheet.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 17. Empty and rinse the cup as directed.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Keep the bulb below the level of your incision to help the fluid move out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice the amount of fluid suddenly increases or decreases, the odor of the fluid changes. The fluid contains pus or becomes thicker over time. Your drain tube falls out or your incision opens. Your incision is red, swollen, painful or has pus coming out or your temperature is 101 degrees Fahrenheit or higher.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Please watch this entire video before checking the blood flow in your leg. This video will teach you how to check the blood flow in your leg. Good blood flow is important for recovery after the surgical procedure on your leg.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Look at your foot and toes on the leg recovering from the surgical procedure. Compare your feet. Do you see any differences? Your foot and toes on the surgical side should feel warm, be normal in color, and have little to no swelling. These signs mean you have good blood flow in your leg. If your foot looks red or blue, feels cool, or has a lot of swelling, your leg may have poor blood flow.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Does your foot feel numb or tingly? A feeling of numbness and tingling may also be a sign of poor blood flow. Or, you may feel numbness from a type of anesthesia called a nerve block until it wears off. If you are not sure if you had a nerve block, ask your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 3. Wiggle your toes. Wiggling your toes may improve blood flow to your foot.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps 1 through 3 every 4 hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Another way to check for good blood flow is by doing a capillary refill test. Capillaries are small blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood to your toes. The capillary refill test allows you to see how well blood is flowing through these blood vessels to your toes on the surgical side.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Gently pinch the tip of your toe directly over the toenail with your hand.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Squeeze until your toenail turns white. Then let go. Your toenail should turn pink again within 2-3 seconds. This sign means that you have good blood flow in your leg and foot.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps 1 and 2 every 4 hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice. Your foot looks red or blue. Your foot looks very swollen. Your foot feels cold, numb or tingly, unless you had a nerve block during surgery, making your foot feel numb until it wears off. Or, your toenail takes more than 2-3 seconds to return to a pink color when doing the capillary refill test.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Please watch this entire video before checking the blood flow in your arm. This video will teach you how to check the blood flow in your arm. Good blood flow is important for recovery after the surgical procedure on your arm.
